Authors explore both normalization and standardization as scaling methods.

End of course:
{AUC: 0.86 - DT-CART)

Early Prediction:
{AUC: 0.85 - DT-CART Week 10)
Week 10 becomes a hallmark where the concept adoption variables start to really play an effect in the improvement of predictive performance.

Concept adoption only becomes effective after 2/3 of the course. 

Concept adoption feature only measures when a concept is first used.

No tracking of unsuccessful attempts at concept adoption.

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: The paper focuses on understanding how students consume and reuse course materials. To do that, authors present a basic classifier that identifies struggling students. Then, they introduce indicators on when students employ a concept for the first time. The improvements resulted in a 85% accuracy score.

text.content: The paper focuses on understanding how students consume and reuse course materials. To do that, authors present a basic classifier that identifies struggling students. Then, they introduce indicators on when students employ a concept for the first time. The improvements resulted in a 85% accuracy score.

rich_text.plain_text: The paper focuses on understanding how students consume and reuse course materials. To do that, authors present a basic classifier that identifies struggling students. Then, they introduce indicators on when students employ a concept for the first time. The improvements resulted in a 85% accuracy score.

blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

rich_text.plain_text: Introduction

blocks.text: Computer science degrees tend to have high churn and high rates of failure in introductory courses. However, little attention is placed on this fact. This difficulty reduces the number of available skilled professionals for ICT companies. So, undergraduate courses are a good place to start addressing this to, hopefully, get more ICT graduates.

text.content: Computer science degrees tend to have high churn and high rates of failure in introductory courses. However, little attention is placed on this fact. This difficulty reduces the number of available skilled professionals for ICT companies. So, undergraduate courses are a good place to start addressing this to, hopefully, get more ICT graduates.

This difficulty reduces the number of available skilled professionals for ICT companies. So, undergraduate courses are a good place to start addressing this to, hopefully, get more ICT graduates.

blocks.text: Fortunately, LMS and the birth of Learning Analytics has contributed to a better understanding of the phenomena behind learning. LMS and other VLE’s can produce data in enough quantity and quality necessary for this. With this, it has been possible to, for example, identify students in risk of dropping out.

blocks.text: During the literature review, the authors point to studies that have found relationships between interactions with the LMS. The most surprising observation notes that extremely high student activity levels are predictors of negative student performance. Another relevant study is the one of Course Signals taht identified students in need and sent them an e-mail. This system, and others, were effective at improving performance but often are criticized by being one-size fits all solutions and, by definition, probably too general. However, there are opportunities in more context specific domains like programming:

blocks.text: The authors identify other potential prospects: the Blackbox project asked users of an IDE about the collection of their data. Another study points to the use of virtual machines on an engineering course. The VM would store the information and send it to a central server for analytical purposes.

blocks.text: Another set of authors found that tinkering had a role to play in the learning process. Others focused on understanding the robustness of tutoring software. More specifically in the prediction of performance, other authors used DTs to distinguish between low and high-performing students on a Java course → achieving between 70 to 80% accuracy.

blocks.text: Fine Grained Data

text.content: Fine Grained Data

rich_text.plain_text: Fine Grained Data

blocks.text: The authors developed a platform for module delivery. The platform was trialed in 2nd year CS students. The platform SOCS handles content and learning activities. Since the platform is custom made, it allows for a more fine-grained analysis of the activities performed in the LMS.

text.content: The authors developed a platform for module delivery. The platform was trialed in 2nd year CS students. The platform SOCS handles content and learning activities. Since the platform is custom made, it allows for a more fine-grained analysis of the activities performed in the LMS.

rich_text.plain_text: The authors developed a platform for module delivery. The platform was trialed in 2nd year CS students. The platform SOCS handles content and learning activities. Since the platform is custom made, it allows for a more fine-grained analysis of the activities performed in the LMS.

blocks.text: When it comes to learning materials, SOCS features 20 lessons, 8 lab exercises and a simulator. Students were encouraged to explored the simulator. Students undertaking the platform had an introductory course on it and are informed about the data collection. In total, 111 students used the platform, recording more than 9 million interactions and made more than 58000 programs.

text.content: When it comes to learning materials, SOCS features 20 lessons, 8 lab exercises and a simulator. Students were encouraged to explored the simulator. In total, 111 students used the platform, recording more than 9 million interactions and made more than 58000 programs.

blocks.text: Observation of the logs shows 3 distinct spikes in activity, each spike referring to periods before either lab exams or final exam.

blocks.text: Another important use case for this LMS is to test whether a certain concept described as important by the educator is adopted in the coding submission. As an experiment, the authors selected 6 concepts and an auto-grader designed to capture those concepts in the programs written by the students. Some of the concepts are easier to detect than others:

blocks.text: Pass-Fail Classifiers

text.content: Pass-Fail Classifiers

rich_text.plain_text: Pass-Fail Classifiers

blocks.text:
The authors also developed a Pass/Fail on the final exam classifier to apply to their students. To do that, the authors explore multiple different options for classifiers, compare their performance and select the best one. The authors compare the different models using Cross-Validation from sklearn. 

As inputs, the models use data collected from the LMS throughout the entire semester (a prediction is attempted at the end of every week). The variables take advantage of the fact that this is a custom platform that is optimized for programming. Thus, in addition to standard LMS variables, the variables also include successful compilations of code or complexity of the failed compilations. The authors use use a ratio of on-campus/off campus interactions.

blocks.text: Results

text.content: Results

rich_text.plain_text: Results

blocks.text: The AUC score evolves with time as expected, gradually increasing over time. Moreover, authors identify the CART as the best model on the later sections of the course.

blocks.text: More than just making these predictions, the authors attempt to compare these models with other models that incorporate concept adoption features. Their incorporation increases the performance of the models after week 10.
